name: Low-Risk Fever (Adult)
summary: Stable, well-appearing adult with isolated low-risk fever and no toxic features.

triage: Confirm temperature; document onset and duration
Screen for sepsis red flags: hypotension, confusion, RR >22, SpO2 <92%
Assess hydration, mental status, general appearance
Ask travel, sick contacts, immunocompromise, recent antibiotics
nursing: Full vitals including SpO2 and orthostatics if dizzy
Recheck temperature after antipyretic
Encourage oral fluids while awaiting workup
Notify provider immediately if SIRS criteria met
keyOrders: POC glucose if altered or diabetic
labs: Rapid flu/COVID/strep as indicated by symptoms
UA if urinary symptoms or flank pain
*Fingerstick glucose if indicated
Defer blood cultures/CBC/lactate to ER if sepsis suspected
imaging: Chest X-ray if cough, dyspnea, or focal lung findings
*No imaging if clearly viral and localized source absent
Defer CT/advanced imaging to ER if indicated
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h (max 3 g/day)
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h with food if no contraindication
Targeted antibiotics only if bacterial source confirmed (e.g., UTI, strep)
*Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID x5d if influenza positive and within 48h
reassess: Recheck vitals and appearance before discharge
Re-evaluate for any evolving red flags
disposition: Discharge home if well-appearing and stable
PCP follow-up for persistent or unexplained fever
Transfer to ER or call 911 if hypotension, AMS, hypoxia, or suspected sepsis
discharge: Fever control and hydration instructions
Return precautions: fever >3 days, confusion, SOB, rash, stiff neck
Antipyretic dosing limits explained
Provide written return-precaution sheet
followup: PCP within 2-3 days if fever persists

redFlags: Hypotension, tachycardia, or signs of sepsis/shock
Altered mental status or new confusion
Hypoxia (SpO2 <92%) or respiratory distress
Petechial/purpuric rash or neck stiffness (meningitis concern)
Immunocompromised or neutropenic fever
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; sepsis/toxic patients require EMTALA-appropriate ER transfer
Document return precautions and stable vitals at discharge
Antibiotic stewardship: treat only confirmed bacterial sources
Report reportable communicable diseases to Texas DSHS as required

name: Tension Headache
summary: Benign, bilateral pressure-type headache without neuro deficits or red-flag features.

triage: Characterize headache: onset, quality, location, severity
Screen thunderclap/worst-ever headache or sudden onset
Ask about fever, neuro deficits, head trauma, anticoagulants
Confirm prior similar headaches and usual pattern
nursing: Full vitals including blood pressure
Brief focused neuro screen (gross strength, speech, gait)
Dim lights and reduce stimulation if photophobia
Notify provider for any focal deficit or severe hypertension
keyOrders: POC glucose if dizzy or diabetic
labs: Usually none for classic tension headache
*POC glucose if indicated
Defer labs/LP to ER if secondary cause suspected
imaging: No imaging for typical tension headache
Defer CT head to ER if red flags (thunderclap, deficit, trauma)
*Outpatient MRI referral if chronic atypical pattern
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO or naproxen 500 mg PO
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO if NSAID contraindicated
*Cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg PO if significant muscle tension
Avoid opioids and butalbital due to overuse/rebound risk
reassess: Verify BP improved with pain control
disposition: Discharge home once pain controlled
PCP or neurology referral for frequent/chronic headaches
Transfer to ER or call 911 if thunderclap onset, focal deficit, or AMS
discharge: Headache hygiene: hydration, sleep, stress, screen breaks
Limit analgesic use to avoid medication-overuse headache
Return precautions: worst-ever headache, weakness, vision loss, vomiting
Provide written instructions
followup: Neurology referral for chronic or escalating headaches

redFlags: Sudden 'thunderclap' or worst-ever headache
Focal neuro deficit, AMS, or seizure
Headache with fever and neck stiffness
New headache age >50 or with cancer/immunosuppression
Headache after significant head trauma or on anticoagulation
compliance: Urgent care cannot perform CT/LP; refer red-flag presentations to ER
Controlled-substance prudence: avoid opioids/butalbital for benign headache
Document neuro exam and return precautions
EMTALA transfer for any emergent secondary headache concern

name: Mild Allergic Reaction
summary: Localized or cutaneous allergic reaction (hives/itching) without airway or systemic compromise.

triage: Screen IMMEDIATELY for anaphylaxis: airway, breathing, hypotension, vomiting
Identify trigger, timing, and prior reaction history
Assess extent of skin involvement and mucosal swelling
Confirm no tongue/throat swelling or wheeze
nursing: Full vitals including SpO2
Continuous observation if facial/lip involvement
Have epinephrine and airway equipment ready
Escalate immediately if any airway/respiratory change
labs: None required for mild cutaneous reaction
Defer tryptase/allergy workup to outpatient allergist
*POC as clinically indicated
imaging: Defer to ER only if airway compromise
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO
Famotidine 20 mg PO as adjunct H2 blocker
Prednisone 40-60 mg PO daily x3-5d for significant urticaria
*EpiPen prescription and teaching if systemic-risk history
disposition: Discharge home if isolated cutaneous reaction stable
Allergy/immunology referral for recurrent reactions
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension, or vomiting (anaphylaxis)
discharge: Antihistamine schedule and trigger avoidance
Anaphylaxis warning signs and EpiPen use if prescribed
Return precautions: throat tightness, SOB, dizziness, widespread swelling
Written instructions provided
followup: PCP within a few days if symptoms persist

redFlags: Airway swelling, stridor, hoarseness, or throat tightness
Wheeze, dyspnea, or hypoxia
Hypotension, syncope, or tachycardia
GI symptoms (vomiting/cramping) with skin involvement = anaphylaxis
Rapidly progressive angioedema
compliance: Anaphylaxis is an emergency requiring epinephrine and 911/ER transfer per EMTALA
Urgent care should stock epinephrine and basic airway support
Document observation period and discharge stability
Provide epinephrine auto-injector script when systemic risk present

name: Mild Dehydration
summary: Mild-to-moderate volume depletion in a stable adult able to tolerate oral or short IV rehydration.

triage: Assess cause: vomiting, diarrhea, heat, poor intake
Check for orthostatic symptoms and urine output
Screen severe signs: lethargy, hypotension, tachycardia
Identify diabetes or comorbidities affecting fluid status
nursing: Full vitals with orthostatic blood pressures
Assess mucous membranes, skin turgor, cap refill
Offer oral rehydration solution trial
Notify provider if unable to tolerate PO
keyOrders: *IV normal saline bolus 500-1000 mL if PO fails
labs: POC glucose; UA for ketones/concentration
*Basic metabolic panel if available and electrolyte concern
Defer extensive labs to ER if severe
imaging: Defer to ER if surgical abdomen suspected
meds: Oral rehydration solution / electrolyte fluids
Ondansetron 4 mg ODT for nausea/vomiting
*IV NS 1 L if oral intake fails
Treat underlying cause (e.g., antidiarrheal if appropriate)
reassess: Recheck vitals and orthostatics after fluids
Confirm tolerating PO and improved symptoms
Reassess mental status and urine output
disposition: Discharge home if rehydrated and tolerating PO
PCP follow-up for ongoing GI illness
Transfer to ER or call 911 if hypotension, AMS, anuria, or failure of rehydration
discharge: Oral rehydration plan and small frequent sips
Avoid plain water alone; use electrolyte fluids
Return precautions: no urine, dizziness, persistent vomiting, confusion
Written instructions provided
followup: PCP within 1-2 days if symptoms continue
Return for inability to keep fluids down

redFlags: Hypotension or persistent tachycardia after fluids
Altered mental status or marked lethargy
Anuria or signs of acute kidney injury
Intractable vomiting unable to tolerate any PO
Suspected DKA or severe electrolyte disturbance
compliance: Severe dehydration/shock requires ER transfer under EMTALA
IV therapy limited to short stabilization; not for prolonged management
Document intake tolerance and hemodynamic stability at discharge
Provide clear return precautions

name: Dental Pain
summary: Localized dental/tooth pain or simple abscess without airway or deep-space spread.

triage: Localize pain; assess swelling and duration
Screen for facial cellulitis, trismus, or airway concern
Ask about fever, trauma, and prior dental work
Check ability to swallow and open mouth fully
nursing: Full vitals including temperature
Inspect oral cavity, gums, and any visible abscess
Assess for facial/submandibular swelling
Notify provider if swelling crossing facial planes
keyOrders: *Incision/drainage NOT typically performed in urgent care
labs: Usually none
*Glucose if diabetic with infection
Defer cultures to dental/ER if deep infection
imaging: No imaging typically; dental X-ray usually outpatient
Defer CT to ER if deep-space infection suspected
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO + acetaminophen 1000 mg PO alternating
Amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID x5-7d if abscess/infection
Clindamycin 300 mg PO QID if penicillin allergy
*Topical benzocaine for temporary relief; avoid opioids
reassess: Reassess swelling and ability to swallow
Confirm understanding of dental follow-up need
disposition: Discharge home with dentist referral
Urgent dental referral within 24-48h for definitive care
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway compromise, Ludwig angina, or spreading facial infection
discharge: Analgesia plan and antibiotic instructions if prescribed
Warm saline rinses and soft diet
Return precautions: facial swelling, fever, difficulty swallowing/breathing
Stress that antibiotics are temporizing, not curative

redFlags: Floor-of-mouth swelling, drooling, or trouble swallowing (Ludwig angina)
Trismus or rapidly spreading facial cellulitis
Airway compromise or voice change
High fever with systemic toxicity
Periorbital extension of infection
compliance: Definitive dental care is outside urgent-care scope; refer to dentist
Deep-space/airway infections require ER transfer under EMTALA
Controlled-substance prudence: NSAIDs/acetaminophen first-line, avoid opioids
Antibiotics are a bridge to dental treatment, not a cure

name: Medication Refill
summary: Short bridge refill of a stable, non-controlled chronic medication when PCP access is delayed.

triage: Confirm medication, dose, last fill, and prescriber
Verify reason PCP cannot refill
Screen for symptoms of uncontrolled disease
Identify whether medication is controlled (generally not refilled)
nursing: Relevant vitals (e.g., BP for antihypertensives, glucose for diabetics)
Confirm current pharmacy and med list
Document adherence and side effects
Flag any abnormal vitals to provider
keyOrders: Targeted vitals based on medication class
Verify prescription via pharmacy/records if possible
labs: Generally none; condition-specific monitoring deferred to PCP
*POC glucose if indicated
Defer INR/level monitoring to PCP or lab
meds: Short bridge supply (e.g., 7-30 days) of stable non-controlled medication
No initiation of controlled substances for refill requests
Confirm no contraindications/interactions before prescribing
*Defer complex titration to PCP
reassess: Confirm patient understands this is a temporary bridge
Verify vitals acceptable for the medication
disposition: Discharge home with short bridge prescription
Refer to PCP to re-establish ongoing management
Transfer to ER or call 911 if signs of acute decompensation (e.g., hypertensive emergency, severe hyperglycemia)
discharge: Emphasize establishing/continuing PCP care
Provide bridge quantity and pharmacy info
Return precautions specific to the condition
Advise not to rely on urgent care for chronic refills
followup: PCP within the bridge window to assume care
Assist with PCP referral if uninsured/unestablished

redFlags: Symptoms of uncontrolled/decompensated disease
Request for controlled substances (opioids, benzodiazepines, stimulants)
Abnormal vitals suggesting acute crisis
Drug-seeking or inconsistent history
Medications requiring lab monitoring not available
compliance: Urgent care provides bridge refills only; not a chronic-care home
Controlled-substance prudence: do not refill controlled meds; check Texas PMP/PDMP
Verify prescription legitimacy before refilling
Document medical necessity and PCP referral

name: Work / School Note & Physical
summary: Administrative visit for work/school notes or routine physical/preparticipation exam in a well patient.

triage: Clarify purpose: note, clearance, or physical exam
Confirm patient is well with no acute complaint
Identify any required forms to complete
Screen for symptoms that would defer clearance
nursing: Full vitals and BMI
Vision screen if required for physical
Collect history forms and immunization records
Flag abnormal vitals to provider
labs: Generally none for routine note/physical
*Urine drug screen or TB test if required
Defer screening labs to PCP
meds: None typically required
*Update immunizations if needed (see immunization protocol)
reassess: Identify any condition needing PCP evaluation first
disposition: Discharge home with completed note/form
Refer to PCP/cardiology if exam reveals concern (e.g., murmur, abnormal BP)
Transfer to ER or call 911 if unexpected acute finding (e.g., chest pain, severe hypertension)
discharge: Provide signed note/clearance documentation
Explain any activity restrictions
Advise PCP follow-up for any flagged findings
Return precautions for new symptoms
followup: PCP for abnormal findings or further workup
*Cardiology referral if cardiac concern on sports physical

redFlags: Exertional chest pain, syncope, or concerning cardiac history (sports physical)
Severe uncontrolled hypertension
New murmur or abnormal cardiopulmonary exam
Findings suggesting undiagnosed serious illness
Inability to safely clear for requested activity
compliance: Provider must only sign clearances supported by exam findings
Follow employer/UIL/school documentation requirements accurately
Refer abnormal findings rather than clearing inappropriately
Maintain confidentiality of medical information

name: Tetanus / Immunization
summary: Routine or wound-indicated tetanus and other adult vaccinations in a stable patient.

triage: Determine reason: routine, wound prophylaxis, or catch-up
Obtain immunization history and last tetanus date
Screen for vaccine allergies/contraindications
Assess any wound: clean vs dirty, depth, contamination
nursing: Vitals and brief allergy review
Verify vaccine type, lot, expiration before administration
Provide Vaccine Information Statement (VIS)
Observe 15 min post-injection for reaction
keyOrders: *Tetanus immune globulin (TIG) for high-risk wound if undervaccinated
labs: None required for routine immunization
Defer titer testing to PCP if needed
imaging: Not indicated unless deep foreign body in wound (then X-ray)
meds: Tdap or Td booster per indication
Tetanus prophylaxis: Tdap if dirty wound and >5y since last dose
*TIG 250 units IM for high-risk wound in undervaccinated patient
*Other adult vaccines (influenza, etc.) as available
reassess: Confirm wound care adequate if applicable
Document vaccines given and next due dates
disposition: Discharge home after observation period
Refer to PCP/pharmacy for full vaccine series completion
Transfer to ER or call 911 if anaphylaxis post-vaccination
discharge: Expected local soreness and low-grade fever counseling
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for injection-site discomfort
Return precautions: signs of anaphylaxis, spreading infection
Provide updated immunization record
followup: PCP for remaining series doses or catch-up schedule
Report administration to Texas immunization registry (ImmTrac2) with consent

redFlags: Anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction post-vaccine
Severe contaminated wound with systemic signs
Signs of established tetanus (trismus, spasms)
High fever or systemic illness after vaccination
Prior severe reaction to the vaccine component
compliance: Maintain epinephrine/anaphylaxis readiness and 15-min observation
Provide VIS and document lot/site/expiration per CDC requirements
Report to Texas ImmTrac2 registry with patient consent
Anaphylaxis requires epinephrine and ER transfer under EMTALA
